evals.name: single-adapter-port
evals.prompt: In our checkout service (Hoffmann.Checkout.Api), I introduced an `IPaymentGatewayClient` interface six months ago when we thought we'd swap from Stripe to Adyen. We never did. We still have only `StripePaymentGatewayClient` as the implementation. All ~12 callers use the interface. Should I rip it out? My junior keeps saying 'we should keep the abstraction in case we change later'. What's your take?
evals.expected_output: Apply the deletion test and the two-adapter rule. Recommend removing the interface (single adapter = hypothetical seam). Push back on 'in case we change later' as the canonical anti-pattern. Use vocabulary: seam, adapter, port. Do NOT propose new interface signatures — discuss before designing.

evals.name: extracted-pure-functions
evals.prompt: I refactored our `OrderPlacementService` last sprint — pulled `CalculateLineDiscount`, `ValidateInventoryAvailability`, and `BuildShipmentPayload` out as static methods on `OrderPlacementHelper`, all unit tested with high coverage. Last Monday we shipped a prod bug where a discount applied to an already-cancelled order. The unit tests on the helper methods all passed. The orchestration in `PlaceOrderAsync` calls them in sequence. Where did I go wrong, and is this refactor still worth keeping?
evals.expected_output: Identify the anti-pattern: pure functions extracted for testability when bugs live in orchestration. The interface is at the wrong level — helper unit tests pass while the call site is wrong. Recommend tests at PlaceOrderAsync's interface (the actual seam callers cross). Mention replace-don't-layer: helper tests are now waste once orchestration tests exist. Use vocabulary: locality, interface, test surface.

evals.name: thin-repository-wrapper
evals.prompt: Looking at `Hoffmann.Inventory.Data.Repositories.WarehouseRepository`. Every method is one line like `GetById(int id) => _context.Warehouses.FindAsync(id);` and `Search(WarehouseFilter f) => _context.Warehouses.Where(...).ToListAsync();`. Eight callers across the inventory service. Our team lead added it three years ago 'so we can swap EF Core later'. We never have. Worth keeping or should I have all the consumers query DbContext directly?
evals.expected_output: Explicitly apply the deletion test — complexity vanishes if inlined, so it's pass-through. Identify as shallow / hypothetical seam. Recommend inlining. Push back on 'swap EF Core later' as classic single-adapter port anti-pattern. Note the actual seam for EF Core is at the DbContext provider level (in-memory provider for tests) — local-substitutable category. Use vocabulary precisely.
